en.name: Wetlands-based solutions for climate change adaptation, risk prevention and mitigation
en.description: Adaptation to the consequences of climate change is a key challenge in the EuroMed territory. Several opportunities arise to reinforce adaptation by shifting our attention to NbS and among them “wetland-based solutions”. Wetland loss and degradation is a sign that the recognition of the wetland climate services as a catalyst for the improvement of the quality of human life and sustainable growth, is probably weak at the various governance levels.Wetland4Change aims to validate transferable solutions based on wetland conservation and restoration for climate adaptation and mitigation, in order to accelerate the capacities of wetland managers and of policy makers to cope with the climate crisis at local and transnational level, by improving take up of science-based knowledge and governance mechanisms, through assessments, guidance, capacity building and experience exchange. The main project outputs are: two wetland-based solutions tested and validated in 5 pilot sites at 5 countries (BG, GR, IT, FR, ES): for carbon-sequestration and for flood regulation. On top of this a dynamic user-oriented transfer plan, powered by science-based knowledge, will be developed for the first time; directly linked to the long term potential for cooperating across borders. The project goes beyond existing practices through creation of the EuroMED Wetland knowledge base (new knowledge on wetland location, extent, condition, and ecosystem services); the novelty in assessing carbon sequestration and flood regulation capacities of wetlands for national authority obligations (i.e GAEC 2 for the agriculture sector), the experimental transnational hub for wetland climate solutions. As a result, the project will improve the knowledge and the management capacities of the involved stakeholders to promote and plan wetlands protection and conservation as nature-based solutions in climate change adaptation, mitigation and risk prevention.
en.expected_achievements:
To test and validate existing tools for assessing carbon sequestration capacity in 5 selected pilot wetlands of EuroMed territory, to integrate wetland conservation and restoration actions in the climate change adaptation policies.

Το test and validate existing tools to assess flood regulation capacity in 5 selected pilot wetlands of EuroMed territory, to integrate wetland conservation and restoration actions in the climate change adaptation policies.
To develop a dynamic user-oriented transfer plan, powered by science-based knowledge, in order to provide a road map that will support in the long term implementation of wetland based solutions in the climate change adaptation and mitigation efforts.
en.actual_achievements:
The Wetland4Change project has made significant strides in its early stages, with a strong focus on developing methodologies for testing and validating wetland-based solutions - carbon sequestration and flood regulation, as well as fostering collaboration between project partners and stakeholders. 
The project team was fully established, and a structured workflow was created to ensure smooth coordination both internally and among project partners. A detailed schedule for meetings, events, and deliverables was finalized, and the Project Steering Committee was formed to oversee governance. The Internal Management Handbook was developed and shared with the partners.
The project officially launched with a kick-off event in Sofia, Bulgaria, in April 2024. The event included a visit to Dragoman Marsh, Bulgaria's largest karst wetland, emphasizing the project’s focus on wetland ecosystems.
Collaboration extended across borders, with representatives actively participating in training and knowledge exchange sessions, particularly through the Summer School on ecosystem restoration. Partners presented on wetlands' importance, restoration techniques, and how to create effective restoration plans, further building capacity among participants. Project representative participated also in the joint thematic projects meeting in Croatia. 
The work in the pilot sites advanced with gathering and harmonizing essential data for the two main deliverables for the reporting period, preliminary data and technical information were collected. The data collection followed the specific recommendations provided by leaders of Work Package 1 (Carbon Sequestration) and Work Package 2 (Flood Regulation), ensuring that the information met project needs for the initial stages of the assessments.
Two deliverables are delivered: Del. 1.1.1: Preliminary study and technical guidance report for the mapping and assessment of C-sequestration and Del. 2.1.1. Report for the mapping and assessment of the flood regulation wetland service.
The project's communication activities were successfully launched, including the creation of a Communication Strategy and various tools such as roll-ups, leaflets, and an online presence through social media and a dedicated webpage.
Public engagement included participation in local events, such as the Kerkini Lake Run in Greece, helping to raise awareness of the project’s goals and engage local communities.
The project has fostered strong cooperation between partners from various regions, enhancing knowledge exchange and collective problem-solving.
The main outputs delivered are: i) Preliminary reports and methodologies for carbon sequestration and flood regulation; ii) Communication tools and platforms to engage stakeholders and the public;  iii) Training and educational materials developed for ecosystem restoration and wetland management.
The Wetland4Change project benefits from the collaboration of diverse partners across Europe, who bring unique perspectives and expertise to the table. By working together, the project is able to tackle complex environmental challenges more effectively, drawing on shared knowledge and resources to develop innovative wetland-based solutions. This cooperation not only enriches the project’s outputs but also ensures that the solutions developed have wide applicability and impact across the region.

During the last two reporting periods, significant progress was achieved across key work packages (WPs), advancing wetland-based solutions for carbon sequestration and flood regulation. Through extensive research, stakeholder engagement, and technical training, the project has made substantial contributions to the implementation and validation of Nature-based Solutions (NbS) in pilot areas.
WP1: Wetland-Based Solution for Carbon Sequestration
We conducted preliminary research, gathered critical data, and refined the methodological framework for assessing carbon sequestration potential. 
Key highlights include:
Participation in the Technical Training Workshop in Valencia, where partners gained insights into the Albufera Carbon Sequestration Methodology, cause-and-effect matrix prioritization, and survey plans for monitoring carbon sequestration capacity.
Development of the Preliminary Study and Technical Guidance Report for Carbon Sequestration Mapping, ensuring methodological consistency across pilot sites.
Field data collection and sampling strategy development, along with stakeholder networking for knowledge sharing and support.
Scientific review and assessment of wetland carbon sequestration dynamics, strengthening expertise within the project team.
WP2: Wetland-Based Solution for Flood Regulation 
Key achievements include:
Networking with local and regional water management authorities to assess existing flood risk management frameworks and explore the integration of NbS.
Collection and organization of hydrological, ecological, and governance-related data necessary for WP2 deliverables.
Development of a structured methodology to assess the role of wetlands in flood regulation, prepared during this reporting period to support future project activities.
Preparation for the internal technical workshop and online training organized by the University of Malaga and Tour du Valat, ensuring a solid methodological foundation for subsequent research and implementation.
Stakeholder Engagement and Policy Advocacy
Collaboration with stakeholders was a priority, emphasizing the added value of NbS for climate resilience. Notable activities included:
Hosting the first stakeholder meetings in the pilot sites, engaging municipalities, NGOs, and academia to discuss wetland conservation strategies.
During the events, discussions centred on wetland services, climate adaptation, and synergies with agricultural policies and tourism.
Establishing a dialogue with key regional and national authorities to integrate NbS into flood risk management plans and climate adaptation strategies.
WP3: The transfer plan of wetland-based climate change adaptation and mitigation solutions
Knowledge exchange and replication efforts were supported through:
Development of the Transfer Case template in collaboration, ensuring a structured approach for sharing best practices.
Active participation in policy advocacy forums, including the Interreg Euro-MED Working Groups and the Natural Heritage Mission Institutional Dialogue Event in Malaga.
Contributions to the joint position paper on restoration, leveraging momentum from the recently approved EU Nature Restoration Regulation.
Ongoing engagement with associated partners and local stakeholders to promote wetland restoration solutions for agricultural and climate policies.
Preparation of training material for and participation to the Natural Heritage Mission’s 1st Summer School on the topic ““Our Mediterranean ecosystems’ health is in danger. Time to restore it!”

Project Management and Communication
The project maintained effective coordination and dissemination efforts by:
Submitting the first progress report, ensuring continuous monitoring and transparent reporting.
Facilitating seamless information exchange with project partners, the Joint Secretariat, and the Managing Authority.
Organizing brainstorming sessions on key messages, reinforcing a coherent and impactful communication strategy.
Publishing updates on project achievements, pilot site activities, and stakeholder engagements.
The reporting period has been marked by substantial progress in implementing and validating wetland-based solutions for carbon sequestration and flood regulation. Through extensive stakeholder collaboration, technical advancements, and policy advocacy, the project is paving the way for sustainable climate adaptation strategies in Mediterranean wetland ecosystems. These efforts underscore the vital role of wetlands in mitigating climate change impacts and highlight the collective commitment to integrating NbS into regional and EU-wide policies.

During the first half of 2025, Wetland4Change advanced the testing of wetland-based solutions for climate adaptation across five pilot territories in Bulgaria, Greece, Italy, Spain and France, building a shared evidence base and creating momentum among end-users. The project continued to pursue two complementary solutions—carbon sequestration and flood regulation—through harmonised methods and joint validation, demonstrating why a transnational approach is essential in the Euro-MED context. For the carbon-sequestration solution, partners progressed along two tracks. First, harmonised protocols and data workflows coordinated with the WP1 leader enabled partners to assemble site data and prepare modelling inputs for all pilots. Second, targeted field campaigns were launched where feasible to strengthen calibration. In Greece, EKBY designed and carried out the first carbon-flux sampling in Lake Kerkini, building a floating chamber with a CO₂ sensor and collecting measurements at 20 locations alongside supporting environmental parameters to inform Tier-2 modelling of the whole wetland. n Italy, measurements were taken in the Oristano pilot during a visit coordinated with UVEG, resolving early data gaps and improving the accuracy of ongoing GHG calculations. In Bulgaria, the team focused on monitoring and satellite analyses and scheduled the first GHG field measurements for September in coordination with UVEG, ensuring methodological alignment ahead of field deployment. In France and Spain, partners concentrated on data preparation and coordination rather than on-site flux campaigns in this semester. Together, these steps enriched the common inputs that will feed the forthcoming technical report, maps and communication materials on wetland carbon capacity. The flood-regulation solution reached a major milestone with the finalisation and roll-out of a common methodology led by Tour du Valat, which compiled and, where necessary, produced missing datasets for all pilots and shared an implementation protocol with the partnership. A technical workshop hosted by UMA in Málaga in January consolidated key elements—such as integrating soil data and refining index weighting—and an online follow-up in March aligned partners for implementation. First mapping and assessment results were then discussed jointly for Oristano, Albufera and Kerkini at the General Assembly, and presented to stakeholders during the first Living Lab. A highlight of the period was the Living Lab in Oristano, organised by MEDSEA, which gathered local authorities, administrations, researchers, private actors and community representatives. The event combined co-design discussions with in-field demonstrations, showcased the progress on both solutions, and strengthened a shared understanding of how results can be integrated into local planning and management. This hands-on format accelerated learning across sites and ensured that technical choices reflect user needs. Policy and governance engagement deepened in parallel. In Bulgaria, the Ministry of Environment and Water and the Municipality of Blagoevgrad engaged actively with the pilot, including interest in adding a new wetland and initiating its protection, and participation in a national working group on GAEC 2 to link wetland carbon storage with the CAP framework. In Greece, EKBY mobilised regional and municipal authorities and involved a national policy maker through the Network of Greek Cities with Lakes, orienting results toward flood-risk and water-management plans. Across the partnership, links with the Natural Heritage Mission’s Amplification Room were strengthened to ensure that tested outputs are transferred and up-scaled, with UMA coordinating result-amplification actions and preparing contributions for training and clustering activities.
 Overall, by mid-2025 the project has delivered a shared methodology for flood regulation, put in place harmonised workflows for carbon assessment, launched field validation where conditions allowed, and mobilised stakeholders through a living lab that connected science and practice. The cooperation adds value by providing comparable methods across diverse wetlands, pooling modelling and Earth-observation expertise with on-the-ground management, and threading results into policy arenas from local municipalities to national CAP implementation. This collective effort keeps Wetland4Change on track to deliver robust carbon and flood-regulation assessments, a Euro-MED wetland knowledge base, and a dynamic, stakeholder-oriented Transfer Plan that can be taken up beyond the pilot sites

fr.name: Wetlands-based solutions for climate change adaptation, risk prevention and mitigation
fr.description: Faire face au changement climatique est un défi majeur en Méditerranée et les Solutions fondées sur la Nature (SfN) représentent de réelles opportunités pour une meilleure adaptation et atténuation de ses effets, en particulier celles fondées sur les zones humides. Cependant, ces écosystèmes déclinent très rapidement dans cette région et leur sauvegarde, à travers des actions de conservation et de restauration, est une urgence vitale. Le projet Wetland4Change vise donc à valider des solutions transférables, axées sur la conservation et la restauration des zones humides pour l'adaptation au changement climatique et l'atténuation de ses effets, afin d’aider les gestionnaires et les décideurs à mieux faire face à cette crise, notamment en améliorant l’intégration des connaissances scientifiques dans les mécanismes de gestion et de gouvernance, par le biais d'évaluations, de conseils, de développement des capacités et d'échanges d'expériences. Les principaux résultats du projet seront : deux solutions basées sur les zones humides, testées et validées dans 5 sites pilotes de 5 pays (BG, GR, IT, FR, ES), pour la séquestration du carbone et la régulation des crues. En outre, un plan de transfert orienté vers les utilisateurs, alimenté par des connaissances scientifiques et intégrant la coopération transfrontalière, sera également développé. Le projet ira au-delà des pratiques existantes, avec la création d’une base de connaissances EuroMED sur les écosystèmes humides (localisation, étendue, état et services écosystémiques) et intégrera des innovations telles que l’estimation des services écosystémiques de séquestration du carbone et de régulation des crues de ces habitats. En conséquence, le projet améliorera les connaissances et les capacités des parties prenantes impliquées, afin de promouvoir et planifier la conservation et la restauration des zones humides en tant que SfN pour l'adaptation au changement climatique, l'atténuation et la prévention des risques.

en.name: Digital Drought Risk Management enabling the drought mitigation and adaptation strategies for the restoration of the ecosystem equilibrium in Mediterranean European Countries
en.description:
The Mediterranean region is considered one of the regions of the globe with the highest socioeconomic exposure to droughts that is likely to be exacerbated in the future (Gu et al., 2020). The recent Sixth Assessment Report from the Working Group I of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC-AR6) defines drought as a period with below-average moisture conditions, which results in negative impacts on various components of natural systems and socio-economic sectors. Four macro-categories of droughts have been defined by experts (e.g., Wilhite and Glantz (1985) and Mishra and Singh (2010)), however there is not a universal definition of drought nor droughts cannot, due to their multifaceted aspects, be measured or assessed based on a single variable or through a unique index (Lloyd-Hughes, 2014). In this context, not only drought severity matters, as drier-than-normal conditions are often characterized through their spatial and temporal attributes: extent, frequency, duration, and timing. It is therefore of crucial importance to adopt standardized indices that quantify a drought and flag its severity, using threshold-based classifications, according to the level of deviation from normal conditions (Crausbay et al., 2020).
Germ of Life project aims at developing, test and uptake a “drought risk management preventive approach” based on jointly developed and adapted solutions built on commonly defined data, monitoring sources and indices that will support drought risk prediction for the territorial monitoring systems and timely warning of drought conditions. The cross-border dimension of the project allows stakeholders to collaborate in the vulnerability assessment of the territories based on a shared taxonomy and comparable data-driven risk assessment as well as sharing evidence of innovative Nature-based Solutions and technological solutions targeting drought risk mitigation challenges.
en.expected_achievements:
Development of three jointly developed solutions leading to the Germ of Life integrated solution, by analysing the current drought risk management practices and territorial requirement in four pilot test areas (PTA) in four Med-countries 

WP1 leader - UBITEL
The WP2 objective is to set-up, run and measure in real life conditions the uptake of Germ of Life solution in 4 PTA in Portugal, Italy, Greece and Spain, including a plan for maintenance after the project conclusion.

WP2 leader - CMCC
Aim of this WP is to jointly develop an amplification and transferability coordinated strategy to be used to enhance current regional and national drought management policies and defined plans in Med countries. 

WP3 leader - LAMORO
en.actual_achievements:
During the first reporting period, the Germ of Life project achieved significant progress, successfully advancing the planned activities and deliverables according to schedule. The strong collaboration and seamless communication between all project partners, coordinated by UPatras as the Lead Partner, played a crucial role in meeting the project’s objectives. Through a well-managed pre-contracting phase and a detailed work plan, the consortium ensured that the project started on time and without delays, setting a solid foundation for the next phases.

A major accomplishment was the successful kick-off meeting held in Seville, which brought together all partners and enabled them to develop a detailed roadmap for the project. This meeting facilitated the establishment of key governance structures, such as the Steering Committee and Technical Committee, which were activated early in the project to ensure effective coordination and decision-making. These committees have been instrumental in guiding the project’s development, contributing to smooth project management and communication.

In WP1, from a technical standpoint, CMCC led the development of a comprehensive drought indicator dataset, which forms the foundation for future drought risk assessments. This dataset is critical to understanding the severity of drought impacts across the pilot areas and will support the development of new indicators in the coming months. This work is central to the project’s goals of enhancing drought resilience and provides a solid framework for future technical advancements. One of the key accomplishments under WP1 was the progress made towards the Collaborative Vulnerability Assessment Tool (VAT), which is designed to assess drought vulnerabilities in real time. This tool is central to the project’s goal of enhancing the resilience of local ecosystems and communities affected by drought. During this period, Dotsoft and other partners collaborated closely to define the functional specifications and data requirements for VAT. The tool will integrate data from multiple sources, including pilot site sensors, to provide a comprehensive vulnerability assessment for each region. In addition, Dotsoft developed a questionnaire to gather information from stakeholders, focusing on the social, environmental, and economic dimensions of drought. The feedback from this questionnaire will inform the design and functionality of VAT, ensuring that it meets the needs of diverse end-users, including public authorities, researchers, and SMEs.

In terms of WP2 and pilot testing, UPatras collaborated closely with the Region of Western Greece (RWG) to identify and plan the Greek pilot area. Together, they outlined the necessary specifications for the equipment to be installed, ensuring that it aligns with regional needs. Similarly, EDIA in Portugal and LAMORO in Italy have made substantial progress in defining their pilot areas, which are key to implementing the project’s drought mitigation strategies. These pilot areas will soon be equipped with innovative technology to monitor and respond to drought risks in real time.
The communication strategy for the project, led by Ubitel, was another major focus during this period. Ubitel spearheaded the development of promotional materials, launched the project’s website, and set up social media accounts to raise awareness of the Germ of Life project. These platforms are essential for engaging stakeholders, sharing project results, and ensuring wide dissemination of the project’s outputs. The communication team also actively engaged local and regional authorities, helping to position the Germ of Life project as a key initiative in addressing drought risks.

Cross-border collaboration between partners has been one of the project's greatest strengths, fostering knowledge exchange and mutual support. For instance, the feedback provided by Germ of Life partners to the Joint Secretariat (JS) and governance projects under the Natural Heritage Mission was essential in aligning the project with broader European sustainability goals. This feedback also helped create synergies with other Missions, facilitating knowledge sharing and identifying complementary actions that can further the goals of the Germ of Life project and contribute to common targets. These cross-project collaborations are key in ensuring that the lessons learned from Germ of Life are integrated into a wider European framework for addressing climate and environmental challenges.

In line with the project’s commitment to sustainability, Germ of Life partners took proactive steps to monitor and reduce the project’s environmental footprint. Partners have kept detailed records of the CO2 emmissions generated by project activities, which will be integrated into the Programme’s dedicated online tool. 

In the second reporting period of the Germ of Life consortium's activities, significant progress was made across various work packages, demonstrating the collaborative efforts of all participating partners. The consortium held its second project meeting and stakeholders meeting in Marseille on September 16-17, 2024, an event crucial for reviewing ongoing activities and strategically planning future steps. This meeting fostered valuable discussions and ensured alignment among partners.
Throughout this period, the Germ of Life consortium maintained consistent communication through regular monthly progress meetings, conducted online to facilitate participation from all partners. These sessions allowed for a collective evaluation of the project's advancements and the timely identification of any areas requiring attention.
The consortium actively engaged in technical and administrative meetings, ensuring effective coordination and collaboration.  Within Work Package 1 (WP1), focused on data collection and analysis, partners made substantial contributions. Specifically:
All pilot partners provided comprehensive data for their pilot area, including meteorological information, photographs, maps, and other relevant data crucial for the study.
CMCC continued the activity A1.1 by providing a comprehensive review of algorithms available from existing drought observatories and initiatives. In collaboration with project partners and references for the PTA, CMCC delivered the description of algorithms and algorithm in the internal D.1.1.2 which was made available to all partners in Basecamp. Algorithms and codes included in the D.1.1.2 deliverable contributed to the design of the Germ Of Life solutions in A.1.3 and the development of the pipeline for the data processing and calculation of drought n indicators and the implementation of the platform in A.1.4. 
The consortium initiated the development of the "Database of drought risk projects D.1.2.1," identifying key projects and stakeholders. This remains an ongoing collaborative effort, with all partners contributing to the continuous updating of the database.
Partners actively participated in online workshops organized by the WP coordinator, contributing to activities related to the development and co-design of the Germ of Life project platforms. These workshops addressed requirements, technical aspects of climate data, environmental factors, common indicators, platform flows, and crucial factors for identifying vulnerability to drought. Partners provided input on the most important indicators for their respective pilot areas, considering social, economic, infrastructure, governance, environmental, and agricultural practices, while taking into account the habitat and socio-economic environment. Human resources with relevant expertise participated in workshops dedicated to the co-design of the Innovative Procurement Platform and the Drought Forecasting Tool, contributing to discussions on platform flows, user requirements, drought management, scale of analysis, and indicator selection. Partners also provided relevant maps and presentations on their pilot areas.
Within Work Package 2 (WP2), partners collaborated on activities A2.1 and A2.4:
The pilot partners began the process of acquiring their equipment for their pilot sites. Notably, the Region of Western Greece (RWG) oversaw the public procurement process for the acquisition of equipment needed for the meteorological station in the Greek pilot area, supervising the installation and initial data collection and parameterization.Furthermore, EDIA ensured the development and monitoring of the entire public procurement process for the acquisition of all the equipment needed to implement the meteorological station in the portuguese pilot area.
A partner responsible for calculating the carbon footprint (PP10 - INFO) received necessary data from other consortium members to facilitate this analysis.
Consortium representatives also participated in the Natural Heritage Mission activities, including working group meetings, webinars, and the 2nd Institutional Dialogue event in Malaga, Spain, from November 27th to 29th, 2024. This participation aimed to establish new collaborations and strengthen the network for the Germ of Life project.

fr.description:
La région méditerranéenne est considérée comme l'une des régions du globe les plus exposées aux sécheresses sur le plan socio-économique, une situation qui risque de s'aggraver à l'avenir (Gu et al., 2020). Le récent Sixième rapport d'évaluation du Groupe de travail I du Groupe d'experts intergouvernemental sur l'évolution du climat (IPCC-AR6) définit la sécheresse comme une période où les conditions d'humidité sont inférieures à la moyenne, ce qui entraîne des effets négatifs sur diverses composantes des systèmes naturels et des secteurs socio-économiques. Quatre macro-catégories de sécheresses ont été définies par des experts (par exemple, Wilhite et Glantz (1985) et Mishra et Singh (2010)), cependant il n'y a pas de définition universelle de la sécheresse et les sécheresses ne peuvent pas, en raison de leurs aspects multiformes, être mesurées ou évaluées sur la base d'une seule variable ou à travers un indice unique (Lloyd-Hughes, 2014). 
Le projet Germ of Life vise à développer, tester et adopter une "approche préventive de la gestion du risque de sécheresse" basée sur des solutions développées et adaptées conjointement, fondées sur des données, des sources de surveillance et des indices définis en commun, qui soutiendront la prévision du risque de sécheresse pour les systèmes de surveillance territoriaux et l'alerte rapide des conditions de sécheresse. La dimension transfrontalière du projet permet aux parties prenantes de collaborer à l'évaluation de la vulnérabilité des territoires sur la base d'une taxonomie partagée et d'une évaluation des risques fondée sur des données comparables, ainsi que de partager des preuves de solutions innovantes fondées sur la nature et de solutions technologiques visant à atténuer les risques de sécheresse.

en.name: Harnessing RENEWable energy potential for clean energy transition of MED PORTs
en.description:
Despite being the most environmentally friendly way of transporting large quantities of goods, maritime transport, like any other human activity based on fossil fuels contributes to climate change. This is even more true in MED ports, located in the proximity of populated areas, degrading local air quality on top of the emitting CO2. 
To change this, cooperation is central since not only are these issues common but they also need a shared approach, pollution and greenhouse gas emissions not stopping at borders.
RENEWPORT aims to tackle this issue by supporting the clean energy transition of MED ports, turning them from emitters of pollutants and greenhouse gases to clean energy hubs by exploiting the untapped potential of renewable energy sources (RES).
To achieve this, first we will develop a toolkit providing practical advice, guidance and calculation of the potential of RES use for MED ports, based on their energy needs. This will provide MED ports with a powerful solution for planning their clean energy transition.
Then, project partners will test the use of RES as well as green hydrogen in different scenarios: this will not only concretely improve the performance of port operations on climate change and air quality, but lessons learned will also lead to the definition of a tool for ports as clean energy hubs, a solution replicable in other ports.
Finally, project partners will establish a long-term cooperation network in the domain of clean energy transition, transferring results at local, transnational and macro-regional level and at the same time building strong cooperation with other projects having a similar scope.
en.expected_achievements:
To improve planning capacity of policy makers in exploiting renewable energy sources for MED ports for a clean energy transition.
To transform MED ports into renewable energy hubs
To provide an operational framework for clean energy transition of MED ports.
en.actual_achievements:
Within RENEWPORT, nine of the most relevant ports of the Med area work together to pursue their clean energy transition, untapping the unexplored potential of renewable energy as to reduce their carbon footprint and reduce energy dependency.
After kicking off the project in January 2024, under the guidance of the Fundacion Valenciaport, project partners started enhancing their planning competences in exploiting renewable energy sources for MED ports – thus contributing to the first project specific objective.
Starting from the analysis of their territories, partners carried out a comparative benchmarking study, assessing the regions against each other in terms of renewable energy sources adoption and best practices. The study includes the joint methodology for the development of the RES toolkit for MED ports, with potential content and options.
Also, partners started working on their own investments, by developing the technical annexes to be used for the tender notices. Luka Koper and Port of Rijeka Authority already launched their tenders, ahead of the expected deadline.
Besides, the LP as communication leader finalised the project communication strategy, activated the project website and social media channels and produced the first e-newsletter. Project partners uploaded the news on the project on their own institutional websites and hang posters in their premises.
Furthermore, the LP participated in the Kick-Off event of the Green Living Areas Thematic Community in Turin on 10th and 11th April 2024, as well as the online thematic working group focusing on policy on 11th June 2024.
Finally, under the guidance of the LP and Luka Koper project partners contributed to the calculation of the project carbon footprint of the first semester.

RENEWPORT brings together nine of the most relevant ports of the Med area, to jointly pursue their clean energy transition, untapping the unexplored potential of renewable energy as to reduce their carbon footprint and reduce energy dependency.
After kicking off the project in January 2024, during the first year of the project the Lead Partner ensured constant dialogue among partners, also organizing three Project Steering Committee meetings to coordinate project activities. Under the guidance of the Fundacion Valenciaport, project partners started enhancing their planning competences in exploiting renewable energy sources for MED ports – thus contributing to the first project specific objective.
Starting from the analysis of their territories, partners carried out a comparative benchmarking study, assessing the regions against each other in terms of renewable energy sources adoption and best practices. The study includes the joint methodology for the development of the RES toolkit for MED ports, with potential content and options.
During the second semester, coordinated by Fundacion Valenciaport, project partners cooperated to create RENEWPORT RES toolkit for MED ports. Fundacion Valenciaport, as WPL, conducted research to develop this toolkit, which is designed to compare the solar and wind potential of Mediterranean ports. The study focused on creating a tool that allows inexperienced users to estimate solar or wind energy generation for a given area, while also enabling expert users to input detailed parameters for more precise calculations. This flexibility ensures that the toolkit will be accessible to a broad range of stakeholders while maintaining high accuracy for technical users. Based on this, a demo version of the RENEWPORT RES toolkit for MED ports has been prepared and shared among partners to test its functionalities, jointly peer review and evaluate the toolkit. This collaborative process ensures the tool meets project needs effectively. The toolkit has a form of a web tool, which will be uploaded in the e-learning platform on the project’s website and which, based on the port’s energy needs, will calculate how much renewable energy and of which type a port could generate. This toolkit can be used by any port in the MED area – and beyond- and endows policy makers with increased capacities to plan their clean energy transition of MED ports by exploiting renewable energy sources.
Also, partners started to co-design their pilot actions by setting out a common methodology for their implementation. They started working on their own investments, by developing the technical annexes, launching the tender procedures and some of them already awarded the contracts. 
Besides, LP and PPs carried out the necessary communication and dissemination activities to raise the awareness and increase knowledge of target groups on the MED ports clean energy transition through the use of renewable energy sources. Based on the communication strategy prepared in the first moths, LP activated and constantly updated the project website and social media channels and produced two e-newsletters. Also, the other Project partners had an important role in the overall communication activities, uploading the news on the project on their own institutional websites, hanging posters in their premises and attending local events and meeting.
RENEWPORT is also part of the MISSION: Promoting green living areas. Being part of the Mission allowed RENEWPORT to cooperate with Governance projects and share, exchange and develop synergies with other Thematic projects tackling the same topics. RENEWPORT didn’t organize events, but as part of the Mission, LP attended several online and physical meetings and events organized by Governance projects, which were important cross-fertilization opportunities to better reach relevant stakeholders and policy makers.
Finally, under the guidance of the LP and Luka Koper project partners contributed to the calculation of the project carbon footprint of first and second semester.

fr.name: Harnessing RENEWable energy potential for clean energy transition of MED PORTs
fr.description:
Bien qu'il soit le moyen le plus écologique de transporter de grandes quantités de marchandises, le transport maritime, comme toute autre activité humaine basée sur les combustibles fossiles, contribue au changement climatique. C'est encore plus vrai dans les ports MED, situés à proximité de zones habitées, qui dégradent la qualité de l'air local en plus d'émettre du CO2. 
Pour changer cela, la coopération est essentielle car non seulement ces problèmes sont communs mais ils nécessitent également une approche commune, la pollution et les émissions de gaz à effet de serre ne s'arrêtant pas aux frontières.
RENEWPORT vise à s'attaquer à ce problème en soutenant la transition énergétique propre des ports MED, les transformant d'émetteurs de polluants et de gaz à effet de serre en centres d'énergie propre en exploitant le potentiel inexploité des sources d'énergie renouvelables (EnR).
Pour y parvenir, nous allons d'abord développer une boîte à outils fournissant des conseils pratiques, des orientations et le calcul du potentiel d'utilisation des EnR pour les ports MED, en fonction de leurs besoins énergétiques. Les ports MED disposeront ainsi d'une solution puissante pour planifier leur transition vers une énergie propre.
Ensuite, les partenaires du projet testeront l'utilisation des EnR ainsi que de l'hydrogène vert dans différents scénarios : cela permettra non seulement d'améliorer concrètement la performance des opérations portuaires sur le changement climatique et la qualité de l'air, mais les leçons apprises conduiront également à la définition d'un outil pour les ports en tant que hubs d'énergie propre, une solution reproductible dans d'autres ports.
Enfin, les partenaires du projet établiront un réseau de coopération à long terme dans le domaine de la transition énergétique propre, en transférant les résultats au niveau local, transnational et macro-régional, tout en établissant une coopération solide avec d'autres projets ayant une portée similaire.
fr.expected_achievements:
To improve planning capacity of policy makers in exploiting renewable energy sources for MED ports for a clean energy transition.
To transform MED ports into renewable energy hubs
To provide an operational framework for clean energy transition of MED ports.

fr.name: Green and Resilient European Excellence Network for Smart MED SMEs
fr.description: Le contexte socio-économique et politique a radicalement changé, mettant en évidence la nécessité de repenser les priorités de fabrication et l'organisation de l'approvisionnement.Les concepts de résilience, d'économie d'énergie, d'économie circulaire, de pénurie de matières premières et de dépendance deviennent urgents. L'industrie manufacturière peut contribuer à la "croissance verte", en favorisant une industrie neutre sur le plan climatique et circulaire.Les PMEs, avec leur rôle crucial dans les économies, le développement régional, manquent souvent de capacité financière pour investir dans la R&I, ce qui entraîne une faible participation aux activités de collaboration. Ainsi, l'objectif est d'intégrer et d'améliorer les méthodologies développées dans les projets Interreg EuroMED GREENOMED et FinMED en s'appuyant sur la Communauté GREEN GROWTH ainsi que de développer, tester et rendre transférable la méthodologie GREENSMARTMED et la boîte à outils associée.Cette méthodologie et ces outils GREENSMARTMED visent à renforcer la coopération transrégionale entre les parties prenantes de 4helix dans les 5 régions concernées (Italie, Espagne, France, Grèce et Bulgarie) afin de créer une communauté européenne capable d'apporter des innovations pour une fabrication verte et résiliente, de contribuer à la mise en œuvre de RIS3 et d'aider les PME à transformer les défis de la durabilité en opportunités.Le test de la méthodologie GREENSMARTMED et de la boîte à outils correspondante dans des régions européennes présentant différents niveaux de développement économique aidera le partenariat à développer une méthodologie facilement transférable.Sur la base de la RIS3, les secteurs cibles sont les machines, le textile, les plastiques, l'agroalimentaire et la mobilité et les batteries. L'accent sera mis sur les solutions, le transfert de technologie concernant les matériaux et produits chimiques durables, l'optimisation de l'énergie et des ressources et la réduction des émissions
fr.expected_achievements:
Innovative methodology to enhance transnational cooperation, capitalizing existing tools to accelerate technology transfer, turn environmental challenges to business opportunities and support RIS3, SMEs, clusters and 4H international cooperation
Pilot testing of the developed innovative green methodology in the five Regions, by enhancing innovation capacities applying the capitalized existing tools and checking their results in facing environmental challenges detected at the Regional level.
Development of a transferring protocol in cooperation with PA, clusters, 4H and SMEs, to allow the adoption of the project results, lessons learnt and best practices in the RIS3 and the replicability of the tested methodology in new MED regions.

en.name: Green and Resilient European Excellence Network for Smart MED SMEs
en.description:
The socio-economic and political context has dramatically changed in the last two years, highlighting the need to rethink manufacturing priorities and supply chain organization. Due to the problematic geopolitical situation and uncertainty in health conditions, the concepts of resilience, energy saving, circular economy, raw materials scarcity and dependency are becoming increasingly relevant and urgent. Manufacturing can significantly contribute to “green growth”, fostering a climate-neutral, circular and clean industry. In this situation, SMEs, with their crucial role in economies, regional development, and competitiveness, often lack the organizational structure and financial capability to invest in R&I, leading to a scarce participation in collaboration activities.
The objective of GREENSMARTMED is to integrate and upgrade the methodologies developed in the Interreg EuroMED GREENOMED and FinMED projects and, leveraging the GREEN GROWTH Community, to develop, test, and make transferable the new integrated methodology and related toolkit. These GREENSMARTMED methodology and toolkit aim at enhancing transregional cooperation among the 4helix stakeholders in the 5 Regions involved (in Italy, Spain, France, Greece and Bulgaria) to create a European community able to uptake innovations for a green and resilient manufacturing, contributing to RIS3 implementation, and helping SMEs to turn sustainability challenges into opportunities and establish sustainable business development opportunities. Testing GREENSMARTMED methodology and toolkit in Regions with various economic development levels will allow the development of a widely transferable methodology in Europe. Based on RIS3, the target sectors are machinery, textile, plastics, agri-food, and mobility&batteries with a focus on solutions, technology transfer, and business practices addressing sustainable materials/chemicals, energy&resources optimization, and emission reduction.
en.expected_achievements:
Innovative methodology to enhance transnational cooperation, capitalizing existing tools to accelerate technology transfer, turn environmental challenges to business opportunities and support RIS3, SMEs, clusters and 4H international cooperation
Pilot testing of the developed innovative green methodology in the five Regions, by enhancing innovation capacities applying the capitalized existing tools and checking their results in facing environmental challenges detected at the Regional level.
Development of a transferring protocol in cooperation with PA, clusters, 4H and SMEs, to allow the adoption of the project results, lessons learnt and best practices in the RIS3 and the replicability of the tested methodology in new MED regions.
en.actual_achievements:
After an in-depth of the methodologies developed in GREENOMED, finMED projects and in the GREEN GROWTH community, we developed the GREENSMARTMED methodology that, starting from the development of regional networks, has the aim of enhancing transregional cooperation among 4helix stakeholders in the 5 Regions involved (in Italy, Spain, France, Greece and Bulgaria) to create a European community bringing innovation for a green and resilient manufacturing and contributing to RIS3 implementation. The methodology, that is part of the result 1 “GREENSMARTMED methodology and toolkit”, resulted in the following 8 steps:
-	Regional ecosystem mapping 
-	Identification of key topics for R&I focusing on RIS3
-	Establishment of regional core groups
-	Motivating ideas and consolidation of regional core groups
-	Involvement of other regions
-	Ideas’ development and evaluation
-	Assessment of SMEs capabilities
-	Identification of Financial Instruments
-	Regional/Transregional cooperation initiative design
This methodology has been pre-validated in the 5 regions separately through internal meeting with the associate partners involved in the project and other regional stakeholders, in which the methodology has been presented and discussed to assess its robustness and applicability.
In this activity all the partners and associate partners have collaborated in the definition and validation of the methodology, as foreseen in output 1.1.
Furthermore, a regional ecosystem mapping tool has been developed with the objective of identifying opportunities for integration and needs for upgrading, given the current novel socio-economic and political context. Each Region has filled out this Regional ecosystem mapping, with of initial list of the main stakeholders to be involved in the project activities, according to their typology:
-	industrial stakeholders: they will provide bottom-up priorities and contents for establishing regional core groups. 
-	policy makers: they will be the main target of activity 3.2 in WP3 (Policy strategies and Policy makers engagement). In fact, they should direct their green innovation funding policies considering the needs emerged in their Regions through the collective efforts of regional stakeholders. In addition, within activity 3.2 it is foreseen that partners will be responsible for carrying out at least two transfer actions towards Regional/national/local authorities.
-	Intermediaries and Business Support Organisations: this kind of stakeholders, and Clusters in particular, will implement the methodology developed. In addition, they should allow the systematic identification and exploitation of synergies with other MED regions which complement or reinforce capabilities of regional actors in the design of Regional/Transregional cooperation initiatives. They will be the recipients of the training activities related to the methodology and its toolkit as well. In addition, the business support organizations belonging to the partnership, supported by the research and educational partners, will be responsible to transfer results to other business support organizations external to the Partnership and to Regional, National and/or local Authorities. 
-	HEIs and research centers: they will be the stakeholder group which will enable the technological support to Industrial stakeholders, which should be orchestrated by Business Support Organizations. As for Industrial Stakeholders, they will benefit from the Regional core groups that will emerge, and from its potential trans-regional cooperation.
Furthermore, since the methodology includes also a set of tools to support the implementation of each steps, an in-depth analysis of tools developed in GREENOMED, finMED projects and in the GREEN GROWTH community has been carried out and a first list of tools have been defined. The list of tools will be finalized and validated in Period 2. With the definition of the tool we can completely achieve the Result 1 “GREENSMARTMED methodology and toolkit”, that with will be tested in the different Regions in the other period of the projects.
One of the most significant achievements of the GREENSMARTMED project so far has been the development of the GREENSMARTMED pre-validated methodology and toolkit, a practical resource designed to support green manufacturing. The methodology, developed in Period 1 (A1.1) by the consortium under the coordination of UNIBG and pre-validated in the 5 Regions involved, represents a set of steps and activities to design and set up transregional collaboration activities. The toolkit, developed in Period 2 by AFIL (A1.2) with the collaboration of all the partners, offers tools to implement the methodology. It includes a range of documents to assist stakeholders, such as templates for ecosystem mapping and stakeholder engagement, guidelines for forming regional core groups, revised tools for assessing SME capabilities, and financial instrument analysis. Additional resources, such as training materials, presentations, manuals, and creative workshop formats, ensure that SMEs and intermediaries can easily adopt and apply its practices. Validated by partners, the toolkit has proven adaptable to various regional and industrial contexts, making it a key component in fostering green innovation across the Mediterranean. Always related to WP1 activities, an assessment and testing plan has been developed by CERTH, aiming at defining a common format and process for the testing activities that will be realized in the five Regions participating in the GREENSMARTMED project, in order to identify strategic regional and trans-regional collaboration in the area of green manufacturing. The assessment plan, tools and format has been defined in terms of evaluation criteria, indicators, and responsibilities. A common time and work plan, governance and communication plan has been also defined for piloting in different Regions to enable transregional cooperation. Both in period 1 and 2, data for the calculation of the carbon footprint has been gathered by all the partners under the coordination of TEXFOR (A3.4).
Strategic meetings have played a pivotal role in ensuring the project’s results. In February 2024, a key meeting in Bergamo brought all partners together to finalize the methodology and toolkit, setting the stage for real-world testing in the next phase. The meeting in Lyon focused on scaling the project’s outcomes and planning for the toolkit’s transferability to regions beyond the partnership.
All the activities have been realized coherently with the planning of the project main strategy for Communication and RAS, to ensure the overall coherence of the project with its main final objectives, as well as with its role in the framework of the industrial transition focus area of the ISE Mission. In this sense all the communication activities have been realized under the coordination of CUEIM, who is responsible of the creation and management of a Communication Committee: working at the final release of the project website and making all the improvements and adjustments as requested by the JS, and ensuring its regular management and update; regularly publishing and updating the project social media accounts with posts about news and events related to the project activities; planning and releasing the Project Communication Strategy; working on communication material, among which an updated version of the project poster as well as a project summary and a flyer, made available (both on Basecamp and on the website Media Kit) to other partners for their proper use, to ensure the overall coherence of the project communication activities. 
In the framework of activities foreseen in the Communication and RAS, project partners participated in trainings and seminars organized by the Programme, like the ISEC Hub and Capacity Building webinar (02/10/24) and the ISE Community of Practice Training (14/11/24), that proved to be useful to better align the project strategy to the ISE Mission one, as well as in several events, such as the Forum Euro-MED Italia (Florence, 21/11/24) and the MED Talks – 2nd Edition (online, 16/12/24), that allowed It to better focus on the potential impact of the project results both at National and Mediterranean levels. The project results have also been disseminated in conferences and workshops, such as in the International Conference on “Advances in Production Management Systems” (APMS)  held in September 2024 in Chemnitz (Germany).
With these achievements as a foundation, the GREENSMARTMED project is ready to move forward with the next steps. The upcoming phases will focus on testing the toolkit in real-world scenarios, further refining its usability, and expanding the collaboration networks across regions. By building on the progress made so far, the project is poised to deliver even greater impact, paving the way for sustainable innovation and regional resilience.

During Period 3 the following activities have been carried out:

WP2: the activities of implementing and testing the methodology and the tools developed in WP1 were carried out in the 5 Regions involved in the project. Working groups were created in all the Regions by the partners engaging Small and Medium Enterprises, Large companies, Business Support Organizations (BSOs), Higher Education Institutions (HEIs), Local, Regional, and National Public Authorities. Meetings and discussions on the relevant Research&Innovation priorities related to green manufacturing were organized and will continue in the next periods. CERTH as Work Package Leader constantly provided guidelines regarding the specific activities to ensure smooth execution and diligently monitored their progress through online and in-person progress meetings. Hereafter the sectorial focus on the Regions:
Italy: focus on polymers, textile, batteries, and machinery sectors
Spain: focus on the textile sector, with the establishment of the Textile-Forestry Regional Core Group
Greece: focus on the agri-food sector, with CERTH’s core group focusing on wine-making and RDF’s core group focusing on dairy
France: focus on polymers sector
Bulgaria: focus on the machinery and agrifood sector
Furthermore, testing and assessment plan and tool has been elaborated by POL and presented during Thessaloniki meeting to prepare activity 2.2.

WP3 – Activity 3.3: under the lead of CUEIM, all the partners carried out several communication activities to promote the GREENSMARTMED project and contribute to its sustainability objectives. In particular, the partners collaborated with TCP and IDP governance projects, participating in: Peer-reviewing, mentoring, and twinning sessions, ISE CoP Gathering (Sarajevo, April 2025), ISE Mission Communication Network activities. As follow up of the ISE CoP Gathering, the consortium started planning some initiatives to involve “Birzeit University”, the taker assigned to the project under the “twinning and Mentoring programme”, led by the ISE Governance Projects.
Regarding Communication and RAS Strategy Implementation, under the leadership of CUEIM, the consortium carried out the following activities:
-	Annual Communication Report (2024): Finalized and submitted in February 2025.
-	Communication Committee Meetings: Held formal meetings (30 Jan, 27 May 2025) and several internal sessions to coordinate events and participation in ISE CoP and project meetings.
-	Partner Coordination: Conducted bilateral meetings between CUEIM and AFIL, UNIBG, CERTH, EURECAT, and TEXFOR to define messaging and outreach strategies.
-	Website and Social Media: Maintained weekly posts on Facebook and LinkedIn, updated the website, and ensured content promotion for regional core groups.
-	Communication Materials: Updated project media kit (summary, flyer, posters, roll-up) and submitted the final project video (17 Jan 2025).
Furthermore, the Consortium Meeting was held in Thessaloniki, where the project consortium discussed about the progress of the project’s activities, and participated in the capacity building transnational cooperation event “The Green Innovation in the Agrifood sector” organized by CERTH where some partners brought their stakeholders.
WP3 – Activity 3.4: under the leadership of TEXFOR, the consortium contributed to the project’s environmental sustainability goals by completing the Carbon Footprint assessment and contributing to the deployment of the Carbon Footprint report. Furthermore, 3 Carbon Footprint sessions (April 2025, May 2025, and June 2025) were carried out, engaging in discussions on reducing environmental impact.

fr.name: Fire Free MED
fr.description:
Comme justement classifié par le programme Interreg Euro MED, le changement climatique aura les effets environnementaux les plus importants dans le nord et sud de l’Europe. Les zones exposées au risque de sècheresse et vagues de chaleur sont concentrés le long de la MED. Dans le même temps, cette zone connaît une influence anthropogénique très vaste, dans une large mesure dûe à des économies dominantes de la MED généralement orientées vers le tourisme. À l’esprit de ce défi commun, cette zone a un besoin profond d’une harmonisation transnationale d’outils et pratiques pour surveiller et gérer les catastrophes naturelles et les risques provoqués par les activités humaines, tous avec un seul but, la création d’une approche commune à la prévention des effets du changement climatique. Le projet FRED se concentre sur la prévention et l’atténuation des effets du changement climatique sous forme de feux de forêts. la MED est devenue « un point névralgique » des feux de forêts, en particulier, au sens du changement climatique qui pourrait provoquer des conditions météorologiques extrêmes, par exemple, des feux de forêts extrêmes, capables de nuire et déplacer millions de personnes chaque année. 
Le projet FRED aura comme objectif global celui de mettre en œuvre les outils avancés TIC/UAV-véhicule aérien sans pilote de télédétection pour l’ajustement du changement climatique, et la prévention des risques de catastrophes ainsi que l’atténuation des feux de forêts. Le changement porté sera une augmentation significative de la capacité de prévention par les autorités concernées dans les zones les plus vulnérables aux incendies. Ce changement directement inclura six zones de pilotage dans divers pays MED, et va indirectement connecter les parties bénéficiaires à travers la capitalisation des résultats et transfert ultérieur des outils spécifiques, efficaces et pragmatiques dans le cadre de prévention des incendies et de l’atténuation qui seront implémentées dans ce projet.
fr.expected_achievements:
To define and implement efficient and pragmatic tools for disaster risk prevention and mitigation in the segment of wildfire protection and procure equipment for the purpose of six pilot demo case operations in WP2 by relevant public authorities.
To execute 6 pilot actions in order to validate jointly developed ICT/UAS tools for wildfire prevention and mitigation at Kras karst, Rocca di Cerere UNESCO Geopark, Una National park, Municipality of Ulcinj, Lošinj Island and Baixo Alentejo region.
To evaluate the implemented and piloted tool for wildfire prevention and mitigation in order to be able to draw conclusions and integrate them in the Common Strategy on ICT supported Wildfire Prevention and Mitigation in the MED area.
The objective is to generate a network of stakeholders/beneficiaries and to produce, reuse, integrate and consolidate knowledge with the support of TCP and feed into governance projects.

en.name: Fire Free MED
en.description:
As identified rightfully in the Interreg Euro MED programme, climate change will have the highest environmental impact in the north and south of Europe. Areas with drought risks and heat waves are concentrated along the Mediterranean. At the same time, anthropogenic influence at the region is extensive, to a large extent, due to the prevailing overall tourism-oriented national economies in the Mediterranean. With that common challenge in mind, this area is in deep need of transnational harmonisation of tools and practices designed to monitor and manage natural disasters and risks induced by human activities, all with the single goal: creating a common approach to the prevention of climate change effects. The FRED project focuses on preventing and mitigating climate change impacts in the form of wildfires. Unfortunately, the Mediterranean has become a wildfire hotspot, particularly because climate change is conducive to a greater capacity to create extreme wildfire events that harm and displace millions yearly. The nature of wildfires has changed drastically. To illustrate that, in 2022 alone, over 600 000 hectares of this programme’s ecosystems have been destroyed by fires, whereas the annual average for 2006 - 2021 period was around 297 000 he. 
The overall objective of the FRED project is to implement advanced ICT/UAS (Unmanned Aircraft System) remote sensing tools for climate change adaptation, disaster risk prevention and mitigation in the wildfire segment. 
The change that will be generated is a significant increase in the prevention capacity of relevant authorities of the most endangered fire-prone areas. The change will directly involve six piloting areas in different MED countries and indirectly connect stakeholders/beneficiaries through capitalising results and subsequent transferring of the specific, efficient and pragmatic tools for wildfire prevention and mitigation that will be implemented in this project.

To define and implement efficient and pragmatic tools for disaster risk prevention and mitigation in the segment of wildfire protection and procure equipment for the purpose of six pilot demo case operations in WP2 by relevant public authorities.  
To execute 6 pilot actions in order to validate jointly developed ICT/UAS tools for wildfire prevention and mitigation at Kras karst, Rocca di Cerere UNESCO Geopark, Una National park, Municipality of Ulcinj, Lošinj Island and Baixo Alentejo region.
To evaluate the implemented and piloted tool for wildfire prevention and mitigation in order to be able to draw conclusions and integrate them in the Common Strategy on ICT supported Wildfire Prevention and Mitigation in the MED area. 
The objective is to generate a network of stakeholders/beneficiaries and to produce, reuse, integrate and consolidate knowledge with the support of TCP and feed into governance projects.
en.actual_achievements:

All necessary documents needed for the successful management of the project: Management plan, Deliverables plan, Communication plan, Quality Assurance plan as well as Rules of Procedure were prepared and adopted during the meeting.
